nativeRange.habitat: Generally found around the bases of small rocks and coral heads on sandy bottoms at depths of roughly 2–30 m, taking refuge in crevices and rubble when alarmed.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator. Has been widely reported to readily accept small meaty foods including mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped krill / mussel / clam, and many specimens also take quality marine flake and pellet foods. Has been documented preying on small bristleworms and other invertebrate pests in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect, but the same predatory behaviour can extend to very small ornamental shrimp. Generally advised to offer one or two small varied feedings per day.

avoidWith: similarly shaped or coloured small fish

summary: A pale blue Red Sea dottyback with a vivid yellow dorsal stripe. Generally considered one of the least aggressive members of its family — hardy, captive-bred, and beginner-friendly, but still a micropredator on the smallest ornamental shrimp.
careNotes:
The Sunrise Dottyback (Pseudochromis flavivertex) is native to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, where it generally occurs around the bases of small rocks and coral heads on sandy bottoms at 2–30 m depth. It is commonly considered one of the more peaceful dottybacks — Fishkeeper.co.uk explicitly describes it as 'one of the least aggressive of the dottyback species' — although individual specimens can still show territorial behaviour, particularly in smaller aquaria.

It is generally regarded as reef-compatible with caution: it is safe with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but multiple care sources note that adults will eat small ornamental shrimp (e.g. sexy shrimp) and that it is a useful natural predator of small bristleworms. Larger cleaner and fire shrimp typically coexist safely.

Conspecific aggression is generally high, as with most Pseudochromis — keep one per tank, and do not combine with other dottyback species. Pairing is reported to be possible when two very small individuals are introduced simultaneously into a large, rockwork-rich tank, but is not reliable.

A tight-fitting lid is generally advised — multiple care sources describe dottybacks as 'expert jumpers'. Decor should provide ample live rock with caves, crevices, channels, and overhangs.

Sex determination is unusual: FishBase reports that bi-directional sex change has been confirmed for this species (individuals can switch from male to female or vice versa depending on social context), while hobby sources commonly describe it as a protogynous (female-to-male) sequential hermaphrodite. Because the species does not cleanly fit the protogynous-only field used elsewhere, this detail is captured here rather than in `fish.protogynous`.

The species is widely available as a captive-bred specimen — notably bred by ORA from Egyptian broodstock, who report spawn intervals of 8–10 days, ~4-day egg incubation, and 24–28-day larval metamorphosis.

Cross-source spread: max size — LiveAquaria/Quality Marine/AquariumDomain report 3 in (~7.6 cm), FishBase 7.2 cm TL, Fishkeeper.co.uk 8 cm; converged on 6–8 cm. Temperature — retail/hobby sources state 72–78°F (~22–26°C); FishBase and Fishkeeper.co.uk give a wild range of 24–28°C with a modelled preferred mean of 27.2°C; averaged to 23–27°C. Salinity 1.020–1.025 SG and dKH 8–12 are universal across hobby sources. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria, AquariumDomain, and Quality Marine all state 30 US gal (~114 L); biased to 100 L per the tank-size guidance, consistent with the orchid dottyback. AquariumDomain incorrectly lists the family as 'Plesiopidae'; FishBase, LiveAquaria, Quality Marine and ORA all confirm Pseudochromidae. Lifespan is not stated by primary care sources; 5–7 years follows the conservative interpolation used for other small Pseudochromis. IUCN status: Least Concern (2019).
breedingNotes: Has been reliably bred in captivity — notably by ORA from Egyptian broodstock, who describe spawn intervals of 8–10 days, clutches of up to ~500 eggs, ~4-day egg incubation, and 24–28-day larval metamorphosis. Adults are benthic cave-spawners typical of the family: the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female, and the male typically guards the resulting egg ball until hatching. Home-aquarium breeding is feasible by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large, rockwork-rich tank so they can pair-bond before maturing; otherwise conspecifics generally cannot be safely combined. Rearing the planktonic larvae has been reported to require rotifers followed by enriched Artemia and is considered moderately challenging.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pseudochromis flavivertex), family Pseudochromidae, care level (Easy / beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe with caution (may eat ornamental shrimp and invertebrates), diet (carnivore — brine shrimp and prepared frozen foods), max size (3 in / ~7.6 cm), minimum tank (30 gal / ~114 L), and water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12). The dottyback collection index did not list this species, so the product page URL was reached directly.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc, authority Rüppell 1835), accepted common name 'Sunrise dottyback', max length 7.2 cm TL, depth range 2–30 m, native distribution (Red Sea and Gulf of Aden), wild temperature range 24–28°C with modelled mean 27.2°C, trophic level 3.5, IUCN Least Concern (2019), 'has been reared in captivity', and the unusual confirmation that bi-directional sex change occurs in this species.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk (Sunrise Dottyback profile)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for water parameters (24–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025) and for the 8 cm adult-size figure. Source for the explicit characterisation of this species as 'one of the least aggressive of the dottyback species', for the 'expert jumpers' / tight-fitting-cover warning that justifies escapeRisk: high, and for the protogynous (female-to-male) hermaphrodite description (which complements rather than contradicts FishBase's bi-directional report). Used as a swap-in secondary because the designated Reef2Reef wiki secondary returned 403.

additional.name: AquariumDomain (Sunrise Dottyback species profile)
additional.notes: Confirming source for max size (3 in), 30-gal minimum tank, water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), mid/bottom swim zone, and the warning that the species may eat small ornamental shrimp. AquariumDomain incorrectly lists the family as 'Plesiopidae'; this is contradicted by FishBase, LiveAquaria and Quality Marine and is documented as an error in careNotes.

additional.name: SaltwaterAquarium.com (Captive-Bred Sunrise Dottyback — ORA)
additional.notes: Source for captive-bred availability (ORA, Egyptian broodstock) and breeding biology used in `breedingNotes`: spawn intervals of 8–10 days, clutches up to ~500 eggs, ~4-day egg incubation, 24–28-day larval metamorphosis.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species, as is typical of the family: the male prepares a hiding site, courts a receptive female who deposits an egg ball inside, and the male typically guards the clutch until hatching. Commercially aquacultured by ORA from Egyptian broodstock, with reported spawn intervals of 8–10 days, clutches of up to ~500 eggs, ~4-day egg incubation, and 24–28-day larval metamorphosis. Home-aquarium breeding has been reported by introducing two small individuals simultaneously into a large rockwork-rich tank so they can pair-bond before maturing. Larvae are planktonic and generally require rotifers followed by enriched Artemia.
